moveAnnotations.annotation: We start with the Modern Defense, an opening where Black plays flexibly without committing to a specific structure early on. Nf3 controls the important e5 and d4 squares while preparing to build our center. This move keeps maximum flexibility—we can later support our center with pawns or develop pieces depending on Black's setup.

Black immediately challenges our d4 square and signals an anti-establishment approach typical of the Modern Defense. By playing c5, Black reserves the option to support a d6-pawn or create a Sicilian-like tension without committing to 1...c6 or 1...e6 right away. This move also controls d4, preventing us from easily establishing a classical pawn center.

We play c4 to control the d5 square and support a future d4 pawn push, giving us space on the queenside. This move is characteristic of modern anti-Modern setups—we're not rushing d4, but instead maneuvering for a kingside attack while controlling central light squares. The c4 pawn also prepares to support the center and potentially expand on the queenside.

Black chooses the Pterodactyl Variation, preparing to fianchetto the light-squared bishop on g7. This setup is hypermodern—Black allows us central space but plans to pressure our center from afar with the bishop on the long diagonal. The move also supports a potential f5 break later and keeps Black's position flexible and resilient.

Now we establish the classical pawn center with d4, directly occupying the key central square. We're building a strong pawn duo on d4 and c4 that controls important squares and gives us spatial advantage. This move commits to a more aggressive setup, and we're ready to support the center while preparing to launch a kingside attack.

Black completes the fianchetto, placing the bishop on the long diagonal where it pressures our d4 pawn and controls light squares deep in our position. The Bg7 is the centerpiece of Black's Modern Defense strategy—from g7, it eyes our center and restricts our pieces. This is a patient, controlling move that doesn't commit Black to any particular plan yet.

We push e4 to claim maximum space in the center and support our d4 pawn with a second defender. This creates an imposing pawn center on d4 and e4, which is characteristic of the Semi-Averbakh system—we're building kingside attacking chances while maintaining central control. With e4, we're signaling that we intend to play aggressively and push Black back.

Black's queen check is a sharp, forcing move that immediately puts pressure on our king and looks for tactical counterplay. Qa5+ doesn't allow us to quietly develop; Black is checking our king and attacking our c4 pawn simultaneously, forcing us to respond to the check while keeping counterattacking chances alive. This is the defining move of the Pterodactyl Variation—practical and provocative, designed to disrupt our coordination.
